title: 2026/03/30 - EW Subcommittee B
committee: Pedersen Group - Delys Tansley, General Manager Human Resources/Health and Safety - David Wakelin, General Manager Logistics Employers and Manufacturers Association - Paul Jarvie Milford Sound Tourism - Haylee Preston New Zealand Occupational Health Nurses Association - Jessica Clout, Executive Committee
description: Monday 30 March 2026 | Education and Workforce subcommittee B Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill Franks Ogilvie - Stephen Franks, Director Young Workers Resource Centre - Matariki Roche, Communications Coordinator BusinessNZ - Katherine Rich, CEO - Catherine Beard, Director of Advocacy Gregory Eli Aaron loane Horticulture NZ - Sarah Cameron, Senior Policy Advisor - Kate Truffit, Potatoes NZ Chief Executive Healthy Work Group - Dr Zoe Port, Lecturer Caitlin Wilson Energy Trusts of New Zealand Incorporated and Southland Electric Power Supply Consumer Trust - Ben Nettleton, Barrister - Cathie Bell, Stakeholder Manager, ETNZ - Wade Devine, Trustee SECPST - Steve Canny, Trustee, Southland Electric Consumer Power Supply Trust Minerals Industry Safety and Health Centre, The University of Queensland - Professor Maureen Hassall Steffan Cavill-Fowler Master Plumbers, Gasfitters and Drainlayers NZ Inc - Lisa Onland, Representative Construction Health and Safety New Zealand Trust (CHASNZ) - Chris Alderson, CEO - Roger McRae, Retiring Chair - John Hemi, New Chair Port Industry Association - Pat Kirk, Chair New Zealand Thoroughbred Racing Incorporated - Jill Gregory, GM - Legal and Compliance Jodhi Warwick-Ponga Oceana Gold (New Zealand) Limited - Alison Paul, Senior Vice President NZ Legal & Public Affairs - Jackie St John, Manager NZ Legal C3 Pedersen Group - Delys Tansley, General Manager Human Resources/Health and Safety - David Wakelin, General Manager Logistics Employers and Manufacturers Association - Paul Jarvie Milford Sound Tourism - Haylee Preston New Zealand Occupational Health Nurses Association - Jessica Clout, Executive Committee member representing the Taranaki region Business Leaders' Health and Safety Forum - Sheridan Broadbent, Forum Chair - Paul Goodeve, Forum Board Member - Francois Barton, Forum CEO Virginia Burton-Konia The New Zealand Safety Council Trust - Karyn Beattie ProductiveSafety Ltd - Diane Ah-Chan, CEW Port of Auckland Ltd - Rob Mair, General Manager Health Safety and Wellbeing Mel Brown Andrew Haydon

chunks.text: And Franks, hopefully can promote him to a panellist please, team, from 8:00 till 8:10.

Just coming through now, Mr. Chair. Thanks very much. Good morning, Stephen. Got it there. Yeah, we can hear you there. We can't see you, but that's okay. In the interest of time, we can kick in, and I know you know this drill. Okay. Oh, there we go.

Thank you. The bill is very welcome to us, and particularly its intentions, and we endorse it. We've also had an opportunity to see submission by Federated Farmers and Federated Mountain Clubs and others on similar issues on the position on the implications of the health and safety law for recreational users, and we endorse theirs strongly.

To be helpful, I just say, looking at other submissions as well, I'm conscious that lawyers typically are very poor at looking at trading off cost and benefit. They work in a world of Rolls Royce justice, where time and money matters to clients, but not to them and not to the courts.

And the whole climate is of sort of performative virtue, and a view that it's wrong to take account of costs when some right or some other interest is at stake. I mention that because I think some of the submissions that you're facing about the risks of allowing more freedom of action for workers, obviously, but more importantly for us, recreational users, are very easy to highlight.

But the costs of a change in our culture away from a general assumption that people can make their own decisions and take risks and do dangerous things like climbing and mountain biking and riding motorcycles and all these other things, they are inherently inimical to the approach of a law that was intended to protect workers.

And, of course, the assumption is that workers have some kind of compulsion or coercion. They are afraid of losing their jobs or of other adverse consequences if they turn down risk. That is not the case. The law does not need to think of that for recreational users.

They take risks deliberately all the time, so that the starting position should be a carve-out for recreational use. And what we're concerned about is that when the bill was last amended in this area, when the act was last amended in this area, and it was seen as a concession to farmers, it wasn't done very well. My own personal experience, I've got a couple of thousand hectares that I've let a lot of people use.

We had a farm stay operation there that helped defray the expenses of our caretaker. Twelve years ago, the caretaker's family, who do work on a lot of neighboring farms, got briefings on health and safety risk, and they stopped. They weren't willing to continue to operate. They just could not be reassured that they weren't facing crippling liabilities for foolish acts by people coming onto the farm. And I hate that.

I'd love to be able to be generous with that land. So we looked at the bill. I think the primary thing is to make sure that Section 36 doesn't undermine the effect of the exceptions intended in 37. Section 36 wasn't changed and should have been when Section 37 had the farmland or the farm hazard exception.

Another primary recommendation is that for recreational users, there'd be a defense of reasonableness. Essentially, a defense that if this is a risk I would willingly take ex ante, I should be allowed to take it, and that it isn't the same situation as, for example, forcing mandatory helmet wearing on a whole lot of people who probably don't want to have to carry helmets.

But where they are employees, for the sake of the integrity of the law, they've made that mandatory. But for recreational users, there are many activities which have both the natural risks of the environment, but also risks that could be exacerbated, for example, by farm tracks. I use that as an example in the submission because it was one of the things that my farm manager pointed to when I was trying to reassure him that Parliament had given them some protection. He said, "This doesn't say anything about tracks.

That's where work happens all the time. Tracks wash out. We have an open digging for a culvert. It might sit there for three months. I don't want to be fined $600,000 because I didn't put a sign by the culvert or cover it up immediately." And we need to think about these for all of the recreational activities.

So I don't want to use up all the time. If people have got questions, please ask, and I hope that our solicitorAlex Ali Miller is also online. She's familiar with the law and would be happy to jump in and answer any technical questions. Great. Thank you, Stephen and Clark.

If Alex is online, please put her as a panelist. Any questions for Stephen? Stephen, one of the points you've touched on is this challenge with the culture that we are seeing of businesses managing to the letter of the law that's been raised in a number of the submissions, and a concern that changing the act will encourage people even further to manage to the letter of the law, but not actually achieve appropriate health and safety outcomes.

Have you got a comment on how we can strike that balance without leaving the lawyers in the middle of it too much? I think the biggest change, always the law is what is enforced and how it's enforced. And if you had a defense that allowed you to ask the court to consider what a person in the shoes of the parties at the relevant time would choose to do, that would go a long way to getting away from the technicality of compliance.

And there's an awful lot of performative compliance checklists and notices galore, and it's the same thing as the cone culture, the road cone culture that's dominated New Zealand. It's all performative. And if there was a defense that said, if we considered but decided it was reasonable for people to be expected to move past this obstruction without being guided by cones, because there's only five vehicle movements a day on that road, and it doesn't need a stop-go man at each end of the excavation.

I'm talking here of personal experience. We've got five- Mm... kilometers of public road, we're the only users. And now when they have to mend it or clear a drain, they have five people involved, whereas, 10 years ago there was one with a digger. And that was rational because the likelihood of someone coming upon that machine a- and, being injured was very low.

It's quite rational to say you do not need the entire cabal of basically back protection. So I would say the biggest change would be by creating a defense that enabled the calculation of risk against cost and considering it in relation to other risks.

There are thousands of deliberate crimes, we've got an enormous amount of legal talent gets drawn into telling people how to cover their backs against risks that are truly remote and are a simple reasonableness decision that took account of cost and benefits would go a long way.

Thanks very much, Stephen. I think that that's all we've got time for this morning. Thanks, Ali, for joining as well, and I'm sure you'll continue to track progress of the bill. Thank you, Kyle. Members, we are now moving to the submission by Young Workers Resource Center, and so if we can bring them up to the...

And Matariki is presenting on behalf. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, Matariki. Over to you. Kia ora tatou. Ko Matariki Roche toku ingoa. He kaimahi au ki Te Whare Kai Mahi Rangatahi. I'm Matariki. I'm the comms and engagement coordinator at the Young Workers Resource Center.

For any of you who haven't heard about our mahi, we have two kind of main streams of mahi. One is going out into high schools and community groups and teaching young peoples about their rights at work, hopefully before they have a negative experience in the workplace. And then the other arm of our mahi is our help center, where we provide advice, guidance, representation, advocacy to ununionized young workers who are needing support.

So through both arms of these mahi, we talk a lot about health and safety, and we educate and support young people in their health and safety concerns. Yeah, so in our written submission, I won't reiterate it too much, but essentially we thoroughly oppose this bill.

We have deep concerns about how this is going to impact young workers, and we foresee that if this bill passes, young workers will be less safe in the workplace. We are particularly concerned at the reduction of responsibilities for employers with less than 20 workers. Young workers are over-represented, or more likely to be in these small businesses.

And we're particularly concerned about the implications on trainings. For young workers, people don't just learn how to be, are born rather, with the knowledge of how to be healthy and safe at work. They need to be taught it. And then on top of that, we're also particularly concerned about how these changes essentially remove obligations for employers to care or do anything about psychosocial harm, which is of the most concern to young workers.

Those psychosocial harms are the ones that are constantly raised to us.So I wanted to put a bit of a story and some real people behind those words that we've said in our written submissions, and I wanted to share with you today a story of a young worker, who for the purpose of this submission, I will call Chris.

They have been anonymized though. This young worker came through to us within the last 12 months. He worked in a business with less than 20 employees, aged between 21 to 26, and in the industry of retail. Chris came to us absolutely exhausted.

He had tried to raise health and safety concerns with his employer on multiple occasions, both formally and informally, and all of these attempts to raise concerns were ignored. Chris had been recently made a supervisor, but he hadn't been given any training or support for how to look after the people he was supervising and the training he needed to be safe in that role.

He was also expected to do both his new role and his old role without having any increase of hours. There was an understaffing issue kind of rife throughout the workplace, and he was really noticing in the supervisor role that there was an increase of minor injuries, largely due to fatigue and burnout and juggling all of these multiple jobs.

There was also some machinery health and safety concerns that he had that he raised, and again, these were completely ignored. And when it finally reached the point that it was then impacting his mental health, he took it further to his employer, and his employer's response was to laugh in his face. These are the words that Chris wrote to us when he first got in contact with us. He said, "I'm dealing with a workplace issue. I raised concerns about understaffing and needing more support.

When it finally started affecting my mental health, I then raised the issue more seriously and formally. I was direct but professional and respectful, but I feel as though this has been unhelpful and my employer is now engaging in bad faith. It's really affecting my mental health, but I also feel strongly that people can't be treated like this, and I'm unsure of what to do. It's left me feeling really unconfident.

I feel exhausted because all I want was an acknowledgment that the issues I raised were not nothing. I really want some help with my options." As you can hear in that, he's just exhausted. He's tried everything he can to get these health and safety concerns that are completely valid to be heard. So we supported Chris in raising a personal grievance that was successful, and the success of this PG largely relied upon the current act, the Health and Safety at Work 2015 Act.

It was really integral to help explain to the employer why he needed to listen to this. And honestly, the employer's attitude kind of aligned with a lot of the attitude of the current bill, which was that... Oh, sorry, of the proposed bill rather, which was that if it wasn't a critical risk, if it wasn't life or death, then the health and safety risk didn't matter. However, what Chris was saying was actually that it should matter because they were having real-life implications to the workplace.

Through this process, and because of the current act, the 2015 Health and Safety Act, we were able to have discussions with the employer, and through those discussions, the employer was able to understand that the concerns raised by this young worker were indeed valid concerns. That health and safety risks that can seem minor can actually have really serious harm, and that harm can be costly and lengthy to recover from.

And that also having expectations and assumptions about a worker's ability to protect themself in the workplace without then giving them any support or training, in regards to those health and safety risks is unfair and it sets the worker up for failure. This story is really important to share with you today because it highlights the real-life stories and struggles that young workers, and I'm sure many other workers, are already having when it comes to raising health and safety concerns.

And what we're really worried about is that the proposed bill will further legitimize and encourage employers to dismiss these concerns that are entirely valid. We see that young workers already struggle to raise these health and safety concerns with their employers, and we believe that not only will this bill discourage young workers from raising these concerns, but if they do raise concerns, they will no longer have the mechanism or the legislation to fall back on to back up their claims that these concerns are valid and needed to

be heard. Similarly, really worried about how that's going to affect their training, and really worried about how that's going to affect their overall wellbeing and their lifelong careers within the New Zealand workforce. So yeah, kia ora. I'll leave it there if there's any couple of more minutes left for questions.

Kia ora. Any questions from the committee? Phil. Thanks, Matariki. The story of Chris was very illustrative. You footnoted it in your submission, but I wondered if you could flesh out what it is about young workers that makes them more likely to experience psychosocial harm in the workplace, because that's, I think, kind of key to your submission, isn't it?

Thank you. Yeah. There are a whole heap of contributing factors to why young workers are more vulnerable to psychosocial harm. There are a lot of assumptions about young workers. Young workers are also frequently juggling multiple responsibilities, whether that's study, as well as taking care of family, as well as, they tend to be in that part-time work, which also means they're juggling multiple psychosocial factors.

Yeah. There's also, I don't know, there's a really big kind of assumption and stereotyping that happens with young workers that also kind of has this compounding effect where young workers are seen to be snowflakes or they're seen to be not hard enough for the workplace, and so we will often minimize the sharing of the harm that we are experiencing, and then that compounds over time.

Yeah. Great. Thank you very much, Matariki, for your presentation at the hearing this morning. That's all we've got time for, but I'm sure you'll continue to follow progress of this bill. Thank you. Members, we're now moving to our next submission from Business New Zealand, and I think we've got Catherine Rich online for that. Good morning, Catherine.

Good morning. I'm just wondering how I get the camera start. No worries. We'll give you a moment. There we go. No. Move it. We have 10 minutes with you this morning. So that includes any time for questions. Over to you. Oh, thank you. Thank you, Chairman.

Members of the committee, thanks for the opportunity to appear today. May I introduce Catherine Beard. She's Business New Zealand's Director of Advocacy. Business New Zealand supports this bill. Business New Zealand is the country's largest business advocacy organization, representing businesses of all sizes across all sectors.

In forming our position, we have drawn on nationwide feedback from small business focus groups, which was reflected in our report, Reducing Compliance Burden on New Zealand Small Businesses, as well as MBIE's nationwide consultation through its Have Your Say on Health and Safety Roadshow.

And we've also recently tested a response with a number of small business leaders. Health and safety in New Zealand workplaces is a core part of our advocacy work. That is why maintaining a strong and clear duty of care on all employers was fundamental to our support of this bill. At its heart, this bill is about making New Zealand's health and safety system work better in practice, particularly for small business.

And that reflects exactly what we have heard on the ground. We are clear at the outset, the goal is not less health and safety, it is smarter, more proportionate health and safety. Nothing in this bill removes the fundamental duty of care on any business to provide a safe workplace. That obligation remains unchanged, as it should. What this bill does is right-size how that duty is applied, especially for small, low-risk businesses. Through our work, we heard consistent feedback.

The system for small businesses is too complex. There is uncertainty about what good looks like, and there is a real fear of getting it wrong. That fear has consequences. Small business owners told us it leads to over-compliance, a reliance on consultants, and a focus on paperwork rather than real risk.

One restaurant and hospitality advocate told me that some small cafes and restaurants can spend thousands of dollars on consultants, only to be told coffee is hot, knives are sharp, and to watch out for trips. That's not improving safety. That's a system that, in some circumstances, has become over-engineered and disconnected from common sense. This bill introduces a clearer focus on critical risks, the risks that can cause serious harm. It also strengthens practical guidance through approved codes of practice, which will bring greater certainty, particularly for

small business. It reduces duplication across regulatory systems and provides greater clarity around duty and responsibilities. Business New Zealand supports all of these changes, because risk is not evenly distributed. A small PR firm, insurance office, or cafe does not face the same risks as a construction site or a factory.

Treating them the same adds costs, adds confusion, and does not improve safety outcomes. When compliance becomes too complex, small businesses can disengage or outsource responsibility. Small businesses have given us feedback about generic templates, box ticking, and systems that exist on paper but not in practice.

That is not good health and safety. Good safety comes from understanding your risks and managing them, not from navigating complexity. Small businesses are not asking for lower standards of safety. They are asking for clarity, simplicity, and confidence that they are meeting their obligations.

As one business told us, "We would really benefit if you just tell us what we need to do."They do not want to rely on a compliance industry to interpret their obligations. For low-risk small businesses, it makes sense to focus on the critical risks. It is important to emphasize high-risk sectors will continue to face robust obligations. Critical risks for all must be identified and managed. Worker safety remains paramount. This reform is about aligning effort with actual risk, not reducing protection.

And to be clear, this is not about trivializing everyday risks. It is about making sure the system is simple enough to be applied effectively in real workplaces. Across the business community, there is broad agreement on the problem, complexity, and uncertainty. Where views might differ is how best to solve it.

Business New Zealand's view is that this bill is the right direction, provided it delivers clarity and practicality for those applying it day to day. Chair and members, this bill is a practical response to what we have heard directly from small businesses across New Zealand.

This bill does not lower standards, it makes them workable. If we get this right, small businesses will spend less time on paperwork and more time doing what matters most, keeping their people safe and running their businesses. Small business owners understand their responsibility to keep employees safe. Asking small, low-risk businesses to focus on critical risks is proportionate, practical, and sensible.

Thank you to the committee, and we welcome your comments and questions. Thanks very much, Catherine. First question from Grant McCallum. Yeah, good morning, Catherine. One of the issues that's come up is, and you've mentioned it here in your submission, is where you've got a small business operating a large business site, right, and they're operating at potentially different levels of health and safety, what is your suggestion then to address that?

You talk about here extra guidance may be needed. What are you thinking of in that space? Well, when larger businesses have a large workplace, such as a construction site or a factory, they have very high standards, very high requirements, and you agree to uphold those requirements and fulfill health and safety responsibilities as per the requirements of those businesses, i.e., the customers of the small business.

So we haven't really seen any evidence that that would change, because say, for example, an architect is visiting a large construction site. They will turn up to that site, they will put their hard hat on, they will put their boots on, they will sign in, they will often have a safety induction. So the safety in those premises remains the same. So, Kathryn, you might like to add.

Yeah. I think it's just we're not suggesting any legislative tweaks. We just think it's a communication issue. I think we have heard through some of the feedback that there may be some confusion as to whether the bigger company's responsible for the architect's office, I guess. They're responsible in a low-risk environment. If you think about it, an architect is sort of running a paper-based business or is on computers and things.
The question is, is the big

site that has more risks, are they then responsible, do they have any responsibility for that architect when they're working back in their office? We would say no, but there's a bit of confusion around that, so we just think it's a communication and a clarity issue, and that could probably be done on websites and things.

Thank you. Mike- No, go on. Sorry, Grant. Quick- No, no. Go on... through your time. Go on. Yeah. Mike Davidson, your question. Sure. Thank you, Catherine, for your submission. Just wondering if your view on mental health and workplace management is a critical risk.

Certainly, it is something that all employers are aware of. Look, it could be if mental health does develop in a workplace, these are issues of management and good leadership. Certainly, within a workplace, these are things that employers do consider, and they will work extremely hard to provide safe workplaces for all aspects of safety.

In terms of managing the critical risks, though, for small businesses such as cafes, small retail shops, et cetera, these are issues that good managers are very aware of, so issues of mental health, stress, et cetera. Look, thank you, Catherine. I think that's all we've got time for this morning.
Bill, I'll give you the first question in the next round. If we can move to Gregory Lowney, and we'll see you again, I'm sure, Catherine. Thank you.

While we're waiting for Gregory to come up, just to confirm for the committee's minutes, that from 11:00 a.m., by agreement of the committee, Phil Twyford will chair the committee through the end of the meeting. Morning, Gregory. We have five minutes with you this morning, including any time for questions.

I'll hand over to you Thank you. Good morning, and thank you, Mr. Chair and members of the committee. My name is Gregory Ioane, and I'm speaking from a frontline security perspective. I support this bill in principle. Moving toward clearer rules, less unnecessary compliance, and a stronger focus on serious harm is the right direction.

But my main message is simple. For the security sector, we must make sure that focusing on critical risk does not overlook the most common risk we face, which is violence. In this job, violence is not rare. It's not theoretical. It's something security deal with regularly.

Security officers work every day in supermarkets, hospitals, courts, bars, and public spaces. Security officers deal with theft, aggression, intoxication, and mental health situations. Often, security are the first to step in when something goes wrong.

So when we talk about critical risk in this sector, we are talking about assaults, weapons, group violence, and workers being left alone without support. The idea of critical risk in this bill is a good step, but it only works if it is clearly understood and applied. If there is any uncertainty, if violence is not treated as a top risk, then the reform risks missing what really matters for this workforce.

My first point is this: violence against frontline security workers should be clearly recognized as a critical risk. My second point is about small businesses. The bill reduces some requirements for smaller operators, which can make sense, but in this industry, that can create problems and risks.

A lot of security work is done by smaller companies and subcontractors. There is a real risk that violence can become seen as just part of the job and not properly managed. If a guard is sent alone into a high-risk situation without good communication, backup, or proper equipment, that is not efficiency, it is putting them at risk of serious harm.

So the principle must be clear. If there is a serious risk, it is a critical risk no matter the size of the business. My third point is about what good safety practice looks like. We already know the risks and we already know what works to manage them. Avoiding lone workers in high-risk situations, reliable communication and duress systems, proper protective equipment, clear de-escalation procedures, and good training and supervision.

These are not- Gregory, can I just-- Do you mind if I interrupt and just say we've read the submission. Is there anything you want to add to the submission before I give an opportunity for one or two questions? Otherwise, you'll run out of time. I think we can run straight to the questions if you want the questions.

It's pretty much the same. Yeah, it might just be a more valuable way of, yeah, of getting some- Indeed... insight for the team. Phil, did you have a question on this one? Sure. Thanks, Chair. Gregory, thanks for the submission. I think the issue about the risk of violence for people in the security industry is extremely important.

What's your reading of the bill as it stands, and whether or not that risk of violence is brought under the umbrella of critical risk? Sorry, Honorable Phil Twyford. Can you re-ask me the question again? Yeah. Is it your understanding that the risk of violence faced by people in the security industry is covered under the definition of critical risk in the bill?

I think that it is covered, but it's only covered if it's managed properly. Yeah. Okay. And we've got the resources, and we have the resources. Currently, Phil, we haven't got the resources. Mm. It's the resources. We're undertrained. Yep. This industry's outdated. I mean, we just look at the consist-- I mean, just last year we had a fatality in this industry.

Mm. I mean, in the same year, we had another female staff-- I mean, it's just at Countdown. I mean, they're just walking out of the store with these goods, and it's crime on one hand, and then we're trained to intervene and just do the right thing. I mean, they just turn around and assault us. I mean, it's just- Mm. Yeah. Can I ask a follow-up, Chair?

Yeah. Thanks, Phil. Yeah. We'll give you a quick follow-up. Yeah. So, as you've noticed, as you've said in your submission, Gregory, the bill basically exempts small businesses under 20 employees from having to be responsible for managing the health and safety risks associated with the sort of psychosocial. And yet we know for a lot of security people, stress caused by the work is a major factor for people.

Do you not think that the majority of security industries who are small SMEs, small businesses under 20 employees, do you not think they should be covered by those psychosocial risks? No, I think they should be covered by those psychosocial risks. You do? I do. Yeah. Okay.

Look, Gregory, thank you very much for your submission this morning. Okay. You've made some really important points for us, and your submission and the way you tied it to the values of a particular party to make us think about the questions was, I thought, very clever. So well done. Thank you. Thanks very much for your submission this morning. No worries.

We're now going to invite Horticulture New Zealand for 10 minutes, including time for questions. We've got Sarah Cameron and Kate. Morning, Sarah. Hi. Good morning, everybody, and thank you for the opportunity presented today. My name is Sarah Cameron, and I lead the policy work on health and safety matters at HortNZ.

With me is Kate Truffitt, who is the chief executive of Potatoes New Zealand. Kate has previously chaired the Kiwi Fruit Industry Health and Safety Committee and the HortNZ Health and Safety Council, and I have sat on be- both of those with her. We have taken our submission as read and are generally supportive of the proposed changes. However, one of our key points is that we consider greater clarity is needed on how the definition of a small PCBU applies where multiple PCBU's operate at the same workplace, such as where

growers engage contractors or labor hire companies. I'm just going to hand over to Kate now, who will quickly talk more about this, and then we can move on to questions. Thank you. Kate. Good morning. I just wanted to talk you through a couple of examples to make this more real. So farms and orchards can have a lot of different workers on them, and these workers can have lots of different duties, so mixed duties, but they also can be employed by different PCBUs. So we're concerned that there's not enough maybe clarity within the

legislation on the application of a small PCBU and how these can work together. We're leaning a lot on codes of practices here, and we have a live example at the moment where we have been working with WorkSafe on a code of practice. But they have chosen to actually make horticulture out of the scope of this particular codes of practice.

This is roles and responsibility which this would fall squarely on, which seems quite ridiculous. I represent potato growers, we are mixed farming and quite often are grown with arable or with farm animals. So I can't see how you could apply a code of practice to one part of your business, but not the other.

So I think when it comes to developing these guidance, which this legislation will lean on, it's going to be incredibly important to make sure that all of industry is covered and not just pick and choose which piece of industry is covered. Thank you. Handing back to Sarah.

Thanks. Thanks very much. All right. Sarah, yeah? Yep, just invite any questions. Thanks. Great. Thank you. Questions from members. I'll kick off with two questions. One is, in your submission, you talk about the calculation of when you're under and over 20 people. One of the understandings I have of my reading is that if you expect to be under that, and therefore a small PCBU, but then during the season, you employ more staff, you go over that 20. From that date is when you would then be considered a large PCBU, and the idea being that you would then have

to lift your game, so to speak. Can you talk me through how practical you think that would be for your members in reality? Well, it is practical, and we do support that change. It's just that with it needs to come appropriate guidance on how you determine what a small PCBU is, and Kate can probably give further examples. But say in the kiwi fruit industry, for a period of time longer than three months, you would have your core orchard staff, but you would also employ labor contractors, RSE workers, which would move you over that

20 threshold. And so in effect, you would be classed as a large PCBU even though for a majority of the year you're under that 20, but say for four months you're not. And so for us, it's about, well, how do you count? Do you count other PCBU's that are on your orchard at the same time as your core staff? Is it just the core staff that you count? Kate, do you have any more insight into that?

No, I agree. I think really it's got to be very clear on whose workers are whose and what is counted in those numbers. Otherwise, it'll be incredibly confusing, and the reality is is if you're one of those business, you'd just have to set yourself up for the higher standard, because you're going to be going in and out of the number of employees that you have. So you may as well go for gold standard.

Great, and very quickly, the second question is related to your point about codes of practice, and different industries. Take, for example, maybe a farm I'm thinking of in our electorate. Clearly agriculture, got some horticulture, but then also has a butcher on site as well. How would you most practically deal with, say, three different codes of practice in that scenario applying to that site? What would-Be your thought process on how we should think about that in terms of the legislation.

Yeah. This is where some of the confusion's going to come in. It's also in the legislation where we've got different legislation can apply. It's understanding what legislation trumps what legislation is going to be really key in this. So we need to be very, very clear.

The codes of practice, so if you're a farming operation, you would roles and responsibilities, you would lean on the roles and responsibility. So our recommendation is that more clarity is provided for how worker participation obligations apply in multi-PCBU environments, particularly in the construction sector where this is very common.

Our second point is regard the shift towards prioritizing critical risks. So the bill provides a clear focus on life-threatening hazards deemed critical risks. So, in this case, we recommend the bill recognize the importance of identifying and managing non-critical but common risks, particularly for small PCBUs.

Our third point is regarding ensuring small businesses understand their obligations. For many small firms, the combination of running a business, managing staff, meeting regulatory requirements can be overwhelming. So introducing new categories and criteria, such as the small PCBU criteria I touched on before, may increase that confusion unless accompanied by clear, practical instruction.

So that's just a bit of a hark back to the point earlier about just that clear, making sure those divisions are clear and those responsibilities are clear. Just to touch on prequalification systems, we believe the amendment bill provides an opportunity to actually address another key challenge faced by our members, which relates to the inconsistent prequalification requirements imposed by clients and principal contractors.

Many large firms use international frameworks, often from the UK and Australia, which don't always align with New Zealand standards. An example of this is differing PPE requirements for identical tasks depending on the client, the requirement to purchase additional equipment to satisfy site-specific policy, rather than legislative necessity.

So this leads to increased administration workload and costs, particularly for small businesses. And we would just recommend the development of a nationally recognized health and safety prequalification framework to provide consistency across the sector. Finally, a note on enforcement and accountability. We support strong enforcement for deliberate non-compliance.

Businesses that take health and safety seriously invest heavily in training, equipment, and proper maintenance. They should not be placed at a commercial disadvantage compared to those who cut corners. In saying that, enforcement must be balanced with education. So many small business owners underestimate the seriousness of their obligations, and we recommend that WorkSafe increase proactive engagement with high-risk trades so that business owners understand both their responsibilities and the consequences of failing

to meet them. So in summary, to ensure the bill delivers meaningful improvements, Master Plumbers recommends clarifying obligations for small PCBUs operating in multi-PCBU environments, ensuring non-critical but common risks are not deprioritized, providing clear practical implementation guidance for small businesses, developing a consistent health and safety prequalification framework, and strengthening WorkSafe's proactive engagement with high-risk trades.
